text: is what I was becoming, after Ronin took me home . The voices grew louder as they emerged from the tunnel into a huge room. There were dozens of people all around, engaged in various activities — many of which Lara had no name for — and conversations. Pictures flickered on screens here and there on the walls, and machinery thrummed, buzzed, and clanked . “There’re so many people,” Lara said, eyes shifting from face to face . “Not all human,” Ronin said . “There are bots here , too ?” “I’ve counted thirty-three, but I know there are more .” She looked over the crowd, spotting a few skinless bots. If there were more synths present, she couldn’t tell them apart from the humans. They all spoke with their hands as much as their mouths, laughed together, wore their emotions on their faces . It was nothing like Cheyenne. These people were not divided . They lived in harmony . A line of six men in matching clothes marched out of a tunnel ahead, each carrying a rifle. More weaponry dangled from their belts — pistols and knives . “Who are they?” she asked . “ Soldiers .” “Like the gearheads, only human ?” “Not all human,” he repeated, “and not much like the gearheads. They’re here to protect the people who live here, not to hold them under the thumbs of their leaders .” Lara followed the soldiers’ progress until they disappeared in the crowd. “How many of them are there ?” “I don’t know. They trust me enough to let me walk around the common areas, but I haven’t been able to get an accurate gauge of their real numbers or armament. They don’t flaunt it , here .” She frowned; now that she knew their clothes, she spotted three more of them on the walkways overhead . Ronin turned down another tunnel, and as they moved further in, the air grew moist. The smells of wet earth and vegetation strengthened, and soon they entered another large room . Rows upon rows of plants and trees stood beneath bright, warm lights. The air itself seemed thick with life . Dew drops glistened on the vibrant green leaves. Brightly colored fruit hung from the branches, and vegetables grew closer to the ground. Many of them were unfamiliar to her . Stopping beside a tree, Ronin reached up and plucked a small, orange fruit. He handed it to her. It was smaller than her palm, with a soft, fuzzy skin . “What is it ?” “An apricot .” She stared at it for a moment before raising it to her lips. Her teeth sank easily into the tender flesh, and sweetness exploded over her tongue. It was the most wonderful thing she’d ever tasted . And yet…she could barely swallow it. She swept her gaze over the tree, branches filled with more apricots than she could count, and then over the surrounding trees, all equally full . “ Like it ?” “ It’s good .” “Your tone says otherwise.” Ronin stepped to the front of the wheelchair and crouched to Lara’s eye-level, his brows low. “What’s wrong ?” “It’s a whole ‘nother world down here.” She gestured to the greenery. “I’ve never seen so much food in my entire life, you know ?” He turned his head, following her gesture, and then met her gaze again. “And this… upsets you ?” Lara looked down at the apricot and brushed her thumb over its skin. Before she could respond, several loud moos filled the air . “ Cows ?” “Yes, and more.” He rose and returned to the rear of the chair. He pushed her beyond the plants, toward an opening that led into another room. The smell from within was far more pungent and less pleasant . The cows stood in a penned-off area, heads bowed and tails flicking from side to side as they chewed grass. A man sat on a stool beside one. He tugged on its udders, spraying milk into a bucket . “There was an old man back in Cheyenne who had a cow, when I was a little girl. Its skin was stretched over its bones, and its eyes…its eyes were always so sad .” She scowled at the fat beasts. “Someone tried to steal it one night, and they shot the man when he tried to stop them. Next day, they’d already butchered it and sold the meat .” Ronin was silent, but she could almost sense the questions brimming within him . Chickens wandered closer, clucking while they bobbed their heads, and sheep and goats bleated farther back . Lara clenched her fist in her lap. “I want to go back.” She wanted to scream, wanted to rage, but she couldn’t . Without a word, Ronin obeyed. He rolled her back the way they’d come, smoothly, not jarring her aching body a single time . Nancy was waiting in the Infirmary, along with a tall, darker-skinned man. He turned to face Lara as Ronin wheeled her inside . “Did it feel good to get out of this room, for a change?” Nancy asked. When Lara didn’t respond, Nancy tilted her head to the side, frowning. “This is Colonel Jack Rodriguez. He’s Head of Security, and he’s been talking a lot with Ronin since you two arrived .” “You must be Miss Brooks,” Rodriguez said. “Now that you’re feeling better, I have some questions for you .” Lara held his gaze. Anger burned in her gut; the way this man stood, his confidence and air of authority, reminded her of Warlord . “Lara?” Ronin asked from behind her . “Why didn’t you help us?” she asked, voice level . “You’re alive because we helped you,” Rodriguez replied. His already stoic expression hardened . “Why didn’t you fucking help us?” She ignored the pain of each breath, unable to tamp down her rage. “You left us to them! Left us to starve— to die
